import Callout from '~/components/Callout.astro';

Raspberry Pi's RP-series chips have rapidly become the go-to for hobbyist and education — and increasingly for production. Open documentation, dual-core architecture, $1 BOM cost, and the **PIO** (Programmable I/O) peripheral that's like having a small FPGA built into the chip.

## RP2040 (2021)

- **Dual Cortex-M0+** at up to 133 MHz.
- **264 KB SRAM**, **no internal flash** — external QSPI flash via memory-mapped XIP.
- **Eight PIO state machines** — programmable hardware bit-bangers.
- **30 GPIO**, 12-bit ADC, USB 1.1 device/host, 2× UART, 2× SPI, 2× I²C, 16 PWM channels.

The lack of internal flash is unusual — every RP2040 board has an external QSPI flash chip (typically 2–16 MB Winbond). The chip's XIP cache makes this transparent for code execution.

## RP2350 (2024)

- **Dual-core, runtime-switchable between Cortex-M33 and Hazard3 (RISC-V)**.
- 150 MHz, **520 KB SRAM** + **PSRAM support**.
- **Twelve PIO state machines**.
- **TrustZone**.
- **PIO interpolator improvements** (HMC mode, etc.).

The dual-architecture trick is real: each core can boot as M33 *or* as a RISC-V core, selectable in software. Useful for experimenting with RISC-V without changing hardware, or shipping an M33 product while keeping a RISC-V escape hatch.

## PIO — the killer feature

The PIO is **eight (RP2040) or twelve (RP2350) tiny state machines** that run a custom assembly language, each driving GPIO with single-cycle precision. They can implement:

- A WS2812 (NeoPixel) driver (the canonical first example).
- SPI / I²C / UART at non-standard speeds.
- An HDMI / DVI signal generator.
- A SDIO interface.
- A custom serial protocol your sensor needs.

```
.program ws2812
.side_set 1

.wrap_target
bitloop:
    out x, 1       side 0 [2]
    jmp !x do_zero side 1 [1]
do_one:
    jmp bitloop    side 1 [4]
do_zero:
    nop            side 0 [4]
.wrap
```

Six instructions drive 800 kHz timing-perfect NeoPixel output, with the CPU streaming data via DMA. The PIO takes maybe a week to learn well and replaces a small FPGA for protocol bit-banging.

## The SDK

The official [pico-sdk](https://github.com/raspberrypi/pico-sdk) is **excellent**:

- **Clean CMake build system** — modern, modular, well-documented.
- **Comprehensive HAL** — every peripheral has typed APIs, examples.
- **Multicore primitives** — mutexes, FIFOs, lockouts.
- **TinyUSB** integrated for USB stacks.
- **No vendor IDE required** — designed for VS Code or any editor.

It's so well-designed that newcomers using it as their first Cortex-M project have a much smoother experience than someone hitting STM32CubeIDE for the first time.

## Documentation

Raspberry Pi's documentation game is unmatched:

- **RP2040 datasheet** — 600+ pages, well-written, with circuit diagrams and PIO programs.
- **"Getting started with Pico"** — a friendly book-length introduction.
- **"Hardware design with RP2040"** — schematic-level guide, with copy-paste-able reference designs.
- **"C/C++ SDK reference"** — every function documented.

If you're teaching embedded, RP2040 is the recommended target. Everything is open, every API is documented, every example is buildable.

## When NOT to pick RP2040

- **Very low power**. RP2040 is "good enough" but not great at deep sleep. Nordic, STMicro L-series, and Silicon Labs do significantly better. RP2350 improves this but is still not the best low-power choice.
- **High analog performance**. Only one 12-bit ADC, no DAC. STM32G4 or NXP Kinetis are better for analog-heavy designs.
- **Mass production cost optimisation**. The required external flash adds BOM. STM32C0 or NXP LPC8xx have internal flash at a similar net cost.
- **Mature certifications**. RP2040 is too new for many automotive / medical / aerospace certification paths.

## Production lessons

The RP2040 has been used in real products: keyboards (lots of these), education kits, audio gear, scientific instruments, IoT gateways. Things to know:

- **Reset is via BOOTSEL + USB**. Holding BOOTSEL while plugging in USB exposes a UF2-format mass-storage device for drag-and-drop firmware updates. Convenient for users; ensure your bootloader path handles it.
- **The dual Cortex-M0+** cores **share** flash and most peripherals. You'll spend more time on coordination than on actually leveraging the second core for most applications.
- **The on-chip 3.3 V regulator** is buck-mode and reasonable, but for low-noise analog designs, an external LDO is preferred.

<Callout type="tip" title="If you've never used PIO, start with the WS2812 example">
The pico-examples repo has a WS2812 PIO program that's annotated line-by-line. Read it, change one delay value, observe the effect on a logic analyser. It's the gentlest possible introduction to PIO and pays off for the rest of your time on the chip.
</Callout>
